raw_case:
Primary adrenal teratoma in an infant: A case report and literature review


Introduction

Teratomas are germ cell neoplasms arising from the totipotent stem cells. Retroperitoneal teratomas are very rare, accounting for about 5 % of all teratomas of those, primary adrenal ones are exceedingly rare, with few cases reported in the English literature in pediatric age group.

Case presentation

A nine-month-old female presented with a two-month history of gradually increasing abdominal distention, her physical examination revealed a palpable large abdominal mass. Abdominal CT imaging showed a right suprarenal heterogeneous mass. Oncological resection was performed, and histopathology confirmed a primary mature adrenal teratoma.

Discussion

The diagnosis of primary adrenal teratoma is usually challenging and relies predominantly on imaging studies, and histopathological finding.

Conclusion

Primary adrenal teratoma is extremely rare in pediatric age group, and present a significant diagnostic challenge, as a result it should be considered in the differential diagnosis of pediatric patients with adrenal masses.

Highlights

•Primary adrenal teratomas are rare in children, presenting significant diagnostic challenges.•Preoperative diagnosis of these unusual tumors requires a high level of clinical suspicion and advanced imaging.•Primary adrenal teratomas should be considered in pediatric cases of abdominal masses.•Case reviews provide valuable insights on the presentation, diagnosis, and management of these tumors.1

Introduction

Teratomas are germ cell neoplasms arising from the totipotent stem cells, comprising tissues from ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm [1,2]. They commonly occur in midline structures, most frequently in the sacrococcygeal region or the gonads [3]. Primary adrenal gland teratomas are extremely rare in children, with only a few cases reported in the literature [1,4]. The usual presentation includes abdominal mass, distension, and pain, with some cases detected prenatally [3,5]. Preoperative diagnosis of requires a high index of suspicion and imaging, with surgical resection being the primary treatment modality [2].Herein we discuss a nine-month-old female infant presented with an abdominal mass, following oncological resection, the histopathology confirmed the diagnosis of primary adrenal teratoma. This case report aims to highlight the importance of considering these rare tumors in the differential diagnosis of pediatric patients with adrenal masses. This case report has been written in line with SCARE criteria [6].2

Case presentation

A nine-month-old female infant, with no significant prenatal or past medical history, presented with a two-month history of progressively increasing abdominal distention. She had a good appetite, no symptoms suggestive of intestinal obstruction, and no constitutional symptoms. Physical examination revealed a distended abdomen with full right renal angle, and palpable large abdominal mass predominantly in the right upper quadrant, moves with respiration, ballotable, and dull on percussion. Routine laboratory investigations, including complete blood count, renal and liver profiles were normal. Urine for vanillylmandelic acids (VMA), serum alphafetoprotein (AFP), and human chorionic gonadotropin (HCG), were also normal for her age. Abdominal ultrasound identified a large heterogeneous right suprarenal mass with multiple cystic components. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT) of the abdomen and pelvis revealed a well-defined complex mass measuring 12 × 10.5 × 8.5 cm, with fatty hypodense cystic components, calcifications, multiple enhancing septations, and small enhancing soft tissue components. The mass was inseparable from the right adrenal gland, displacing the right kidney posteroinferiorly and exerting mass effect on the right lobe of the liver. It crossed the midline, slightly displacing the aorta and inferior vena cava (IVC) to the left without encasement. There was no abdominal wall infiltration, intraspinal extension, or ascites (Fig. 1).The initial diagnosis considered was a right suprarenal neuroblastic tumor. Given the absence of vessel encasement and other risk factors, upfront resection was planned. Intraoperatively, the mass was located in the right retroperitoneum, displacing the right colon, duodenum, IVC, pancreas, and portal triad antero-medially, and attached to the under surface of the liver, pushing it superiorly. The mass was completely excised. Histopathology of the encapsulated right suprarenal mass, weighing 583 g, showed cysts partially lined with skin adnexa, with a wall comprising adipose tissue, glial tissue, choroid, and bone. No immature elements were observed. The patient experienced an uneventful postoperative recovery and was discharged home 72 h post-surgery. The wound healed well (Fig. 2), and she is undergoing regular follow-up with routine alpha-fetoprotein monitoring.3

Discussion

Teratomas are neoplasms of embryonic tissues, classified histologically as mature, immature, teratoma with malignant transformation, and non-endodermal variants [1]. In terms of locations teratomas can be classified into gonadal and extragonadal teratomas. Gonadal teratomas are more common in adults, while extragonadal teratomas frequently occur in the sacrococcygeal, presacral, anterior mediastinum, intracranial, and retroperitoneal regions being the least common [7,8].Retroperitoneal teratomas exhibit a bimodal incidence peak: in the first six months of life and early adulthood [2]. Primary retroperitoneal teratomas are rare, accounting for <5 % of teratomas in infants. Primary adrenal teratomas in children are exceedingly rare, with few cases reported in the English literature [1,3].The development of extragonadal teratomas is primarily explained by two main hypotheses. First, during embryogenesis, primordial germ cells migrate along the dorsal root mesentery to the gonadal ridges. Midline extragonadal teratomas are thought to arise from primordial germ cells that migrate abnormally and settle outside the gonadal ridges. In contrast, nonmidline teratomas are believed to originate from pluripotential embryonic cells that abnormally migrated and became embedded within various organs [9].Teratomas often initially remain asymptomatic due to the ample space within the retroperitoneum, allowing them to grow to a considerable size before clinical presentation [2]. Common symptoms may include abdominal distension, abdominal pain, low back pain, and symptoms of compression of adjacent organs. In some cases, patients may be asymptomatic and the mass is discovered incidentally. Cystic teratomas may rupture, causing sudden abdominal pain, ascites, and peritonitis [1]. Prenatal detection has become more common with advances in ultrasound screening [3,5].A part from neuroblastomas, primary adrenal tumors are extremely rare in children. Differential diagnoses include Pheochromocytomas, adenomas, adrenocortical carcinomas, and teratomas each with its own unique clinical presentation [10]. Diagnosis relies heavily on imaging, as laboratory examinations are typically normal since these tumors are usually nonfunctional [1]. Imaging modalities like ultrasonography, enhanced CT, and MRI are crucial for diagnosing adrenal tumors, assessing anatomical relationships, detecting vascular invasion, and evaluating resectability [10]. Benign retroperitoneal teratoma on CT imaging typically presents with well-defined margins, internal heterogeneity, areas of fat density, cystic components, and often scattered calcifications [2]. Metaiodobenzylguanidine (MIBG) is an effective tool for diagnosis, and accurate staging of neuroblastoma but unfortunately is not available in our country [10].Surgical resection remains the primary treatment, aiming for complete tumor removal without damaging adjacent organs, this provides the best chance for tissue diagnosis and cure [2]. A wide skin incision is often necessary for adequate exposure and control of potential bleeding. In cases where total excision is challenging or vital arteries are involved, debulking may be considered. Laparoscopic resection has been successfully performed in some instances [3,11].Chemotherapy and radiotherapy play minor roles in managing these tumors, given their typically benign nature [1]. Complete resection of benign teratomas generally results in an excellent prognosis. Long-term follow-up with clinical, laboratory, and imaging assessments is essential, especially during the first five years post-resection [2]. Our patient continues to undergo regular follow-up, including monitoring of alpha-fetoprotein levels.A review of the literature (Table 1) reveals that primary adrenal teratomas in children are exceedingly rare, with few cases documented globally. These cases span various countries, highlighting the international occurrence of this rare tumor. The age at diagnosis ranges from prenatal detection to 8 years. Tumors present with abdominal mass or distension, with incidental findings and other symptoms such as lumbar pain also noted. Tumor sizes vary, with most cases managed surgically. Histopathologically, these tumors are primarily mature mixed or mature cystic teratomas, with a rare report of malignant teratomas. Postoperative complications are infrequent but can include port-side hernia or nephrectomy.4

Conclusion

Primary adrenal teratoma is extremely rare in pediatric age group, and present a significant diagnostic challenge therefore it should be considered in the differential diagnosis of pediatric patients with adrenal masses. Our case exemplifies a primary adrenal teratoma in children, aligning with the limited cases reported in the literature. The unpredictable nature of these tumors emphasizes the need for thorough histopathological evaluation and long-term follow-up.

CRediT authorship contribution statement

Abdelrahman S. Elnour: Designed the study concept, treated the patient, conducted the literature review, collected data, and wrote the manuscript.Elsadig A. Conina and Mohamed A. Elnima: Provided patient treatment and contributed to manuscript revision.Abdelhafeez H. Abdelhafeez and Faisal Nugud: Contributed to manuscript revision.

Consent

Informed consent was obtained from the patients' parent for publication and any accompanying images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal on request.

Ethical approval

Ethical approval to publish this case report was not applicable in our institution (National Centre for Pediatric Surgery, Sudan), because this report does not contain any personal information that could lead to identification of the patients.

Guarantor

Abdelrahman S. Elnour.

Funding

None.

Conflict of interest statement

None.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 9-month-old female infant.
- **Chief Complaint:** Abdominal distension for 2 months.
- **History of Present Illness:** Gradual increase in abdominal distension over 2 months. No symptoms suggestive of intestinal obstruction or constitutional symptoms.
- **Past Medical History:** None.
- **Personal History:** None reported.
- **Family History:** None reported.
- **Physical Examination:** Distended abdomen; a large palpable abdominal mass predominantly in the right upper quadrant. The mass was ballotable, dull on percussion, moving with respiration.
- **Ancillary Tests:**   
  - Routine laboratory investigations: Normal (CBC, renal/liver profiles).  
  - Tumor markers: Normal (urine VMA, serum AFP, and HCG).  
  - Imaging:  
    - Ultrasound: Large heterogeneous right suprarenal mass with multiple cystic components.  
    - Contrast-enhanced CT: Well-defined complex right adrenal mass (12 × 10.5 × 8.5 cm) with fatty hypodense cystic areas, calcifications, and soft tissue components. No vessel encasement or extension.  
  - Intraoperative findings and pathology:  
    - Mass located in the right retroperitoneum, pushing adjacent structures. Grossly excised.  
    - Histopathology showed a mature teratoma (cystic spaces lined by skin adnexa, adipose tissue, glial tissue, choroid, bone). No immature or malignant elements.  
- **Postoperative Course:** Uneventful recovery. Discharged 72 hours after surgery. Ongoing follow-up with monitoring of AFP levels.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Differential Diagnosis:**  
   - The imaging findings of a heterogeneous right suprarenal mass suggested a differential of neuroblastic tumor (likely neuroblastoma), adrenal teratoma, pheochromocytoma, adenoma, and adrenocortical carcinoma.
   - Neuroblastoma was suspected initially due to the suprarenal location and pediatric presentation. However, normal urinary VMA/HVA (neuroblastoma markers) and absence of vessel encasement or extra-organ extension made it less likely.
   - Adrenal teratoma was considered based on the presence of cystic and fatty components with calcifications typical of teratomas.
   - Pheochromocytoma was excluded as it typically presents with functional symptoms and abnormal laboratory findings.
   - Adenoma and adrenocortical carcinoma were excluded given the size, complexity, and absence of functional symptoms.
2. **Histopathology Confirmation:**  
   - Surgical resection provided direct evidence through histopathology, confirming the diagnosis as a primary mature adrenal teratoma.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: The final diagnosis is **primary mature adrenal teratoma**. This is supported by the characteristic imaging findings, absence of neuroblastoma markers, and definitive histopathological evidence of a mature teratoma with dermal and mesodermal elements (no immature or malignant components).

results.content:
### Resoning:

<step 1> So I'm looking at this case of a 9-month-old female infant with abdominal distension for 2 months. The first thing that comes to mind is that there's a mass in the abdomen, given the description of a large palpable abdominal mass in the right upper quadrant. The mass is ballotable, dull on percussion, and moves with respiration, which suggests it's likely cystic or has some fluid component.

<step 2> The patient's history doesn't mention any symptoms of intestinal obstruction or constitutional symptoms like fever or weight loss, which might suggest an infective process or malignancy. Since there are no such symptoms, that makes me think it's probably a benign condition.

<step 3> Looking at the lab tests, routine blood work including CBC, renal, and liver profiles are normal, which is good. Tumor markers like urine VMA, serum AFP, and HCG are also normal, which helps rule out certain types of tumors that might secrete these markers.

<step 4> The ultrasound shows a large heterogeneous right suprarenal mass with multiple cystic components. The CT scan confirms a large, well-defined complex mass in the right adrenal area, measuring about 12 x 10.5 x 8.5 cm, with fatty hypodense cystic areas, calcifications, and soft tissue components. No vessel encasement or extension, which is positive because it suggests the mass is somewhat contained and hasn't invaded surrounding structures.

<step 5> The location of the mass being in the right suprarenal area points towards an adrenal tumor. Adrenal masses in infants can be various things, including neuroblastomas, adrenocortical tumors, or even cysts. Given the cystic components and the size, a cystic neuroblastoma could be a possibility, but the normal tumor markers make that less likely.

<step 6> The fact that it's a large mass in a 9-month-old suggests that it's been growing for some time, but since there are no symptoms of obstruction or other complications, it's probably been growing slowly.

<step 7> The surgical findings indicate that the mass was located in the right retroperitoneum, pushing adjacent structures, which is consistent with the imaging findings. It was grossly excised, and the histopathology came back as a mature teratoma with cystic spaces lined by skin adnexa, adipose tissue, glial tissue, choroid, and bone, with no immature or malignant elements.

<step 8> A mature teratoma is a type of germ cell tumor that contains mature tissues from more than one germ layer. In infants and children, teratomas can occur in various locations, including the sacrococcygeal area, retroperitoneum, and mediastinum. In this case, it's in the right retroperitoneum, near the adrenal gland.

<step 9> The fact that it's a mature teratoma with no immature elements is reassuring because mature teratomas are benign and cured by complete surgical resection. The absence of malignant elements means that no further treatment like chemotherapy is needed.

<step 10> The postoperative course was uneventful, and the patient was discharged after 72 hours with ongoing follow-up and monitoring of AFP levels. AFP is a tumor marker that can be elevated in certain types of tumors, including some germ cell tumors, so monitoring it helps ensure there's no recurrence or residual disease.

<step 11> In summary, the patient had a large retroperitoneal mass that was diagnosed as a mature teratoma based on histopathology. The treatment was surgical excision, and the prognosis is excellent given the benign nature of the tumor.

### Answer:

Mature Teratoma, Right Retroperitoneum

reasoning_eval.step: So I'm looking at this case of a 9-month-old female infant with abdominal distension for 2 months. The first thing that comes to mind is that there's a mass in the abdomen, given the description of a large palpable abdominal mass in the right upper quadrant. The mass is ballotable, dull on percussion, and moves with respiration, which suggests it's likely cystic or has some fluid component.

The patient's history doesn't mention any symptoms of intestinal obstruction or constitutional symptoms like fever or weight loss, which might suggest an infective process or malignancy. Since there are no such symptoms, that makes me think it's probably a benign condition.

Looking at the lab tests, routine blood work including CBC, renal, and liver profiles are normal, which is good. Tumor markers like urine VMA, serum AFP, and HCG are also normal, which helps rule out certain types of tumors that might secrete these markers.

The ultrasound shows a large heterogeneous right suprarenal mass with multiple cystic components. The CT scan confirms a large, well-defined complex mass in the right adrenal area, measuring about 12 x 10.5 x 8.5 cm, with fatty hypodense cystic areas, calcifications, and soft tissue components. No vessel encasement or extension, which is positive because it suggests the mass is somewhat contained and hasn't invaded surrounding structures.

The location of the mass being in the right suprarenal area points towards an adrenal tumor. Adrenal masses in infants can be various things, including neuroblastomas, adrenocortical tumors, or even cysts. Given the cystic components and the size, a cystic neuroblastoma could be a possibility, but the normal tumor markers make that less likely.

The fact that it's a large mass in a 9-month-old suggests that it's been growing for some time, but since there are no symptoms of obstruction or other complications, it's probably been growing slowly.

The surgical findings indicate that the mass was located in the right retroperitoneum, pushing adjacent structures, which is consistent with the imaging findings. It was grossly excised, and the histopathology came back as a mature teratoma with cystic spaces lined by skin adnexa, adipose tissue, glial tissue, choroid, and bone, with no immature or malignant elements.

A mature teratoma is a type of germ cell tumor that contains mature tissues from more than one germ layer. In infants and children, teratomas can occur in various locations, including the sacrococcygeal area, retroperitoneum, and mediastinum. In this case, it's in the right retroperitoneum, near the adrenal gland.

The fact that it's a mature teratoma with no immature elements is reassuring because mature teratomas are benign and cured by complete surgical resection. The absence of malignant elements means that no further treatment like chemotherapy is needed.

The postoperative course was uneventful, and the patient was discharged after 72 hours with ongoing follow-up and monitoring of AFP levels. AFP is a tumor marker that can be elevated in certain types of tumors, including some germ cell tumors, so monitoring it helps ensure there's no recurrence or residual disease.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> The imaging findings of a heterogeneous right suprarenal mass suggested a differential diagnosis of neuroblastic tumor (likely neuroblastoma), adrenal teratoma, pheochromocytoma, adenoma, and adrenocortical carcinoma.

<Step 2> Neuroblastoma was initially suspected due to the suprarenal location and pediatric presentation, but normal urinary VMA/HVA markers and absence of vessel encasement or extra-organ extension made it less likely.
<Step 3> Adrenal teratoma was considered based on the presence of cystic and fatty components with calcifications typical of teratomas.
<Step 4> Pheochromocytoma was excluded due to the absence of functional symptoms and abnormal laboratory findings.
<Step 5> Adenoma and adrenocortical carcinoma were excluded based on the size, complexity, and absence of functional symptoms.
<Step 6> Surgical resection provided histopathological evidence confirming the diagnosis as a primary mature adrenal teratoma.

<Step 7> The final diagnosis is primary mature adrenal teratoma, supported by characteristic imaging findings, absence of neuroblastoma markers, and definitive histopathological evidence of a mature teratoma with dermal and mesodermal elements and no immature or malignant components.
